original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded catch-up loops in GaugeController checkpointing can be exploited to DoS state-changing calls that depend on _get_total/_get_weight
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Multiple functions perform up-to-500-iteration loops per call to fill weekly history: _get_total() loops up to 500 weeks and inside loops over all gauge types; _get_sum/_get_type_weight/_get_weight each loop up to 500. Unprivileged callers can trigger these paths (checkpoint, checkpoint_gauge, gauge_relative_weight_write). If state falls behind (long period without checkpointing or very many gauge types), calls can become too expensive and revert, blocking reward updates that rely on gauge_relative_weight_write.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This chain has no public mempool so ordering attacks are excluded, but unprivileged callers can still force expensive execution by calling write functions after long inactivity. LendingLedger.update_market depends on gauge_relative_weight_write, so if that becomes non-callable within gas limits, market reward accounting and sync_ledger paths degrade/brick.
deployment_assumptions: time_total/time_sum/time_weight/time_type_weight lag significantly behind block.timestamp (e.g., weeks/months of inactivity)
n_gauge_types is large enough that _get_total() inner loop becomes costly
Block gas limit is insufficient for catch-up worst case
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: GaugeController has multiple `for (uint256 i; i < 500; ++i)` loops in _get_total/_get_sum/_get_weight/_get_type_weight; any address can call checkpoint()/checkpoint_gauge()/gauge_relative_weight_write().

proof_of_signal.lines: _get_type_weight/_get_sum/_get_total/_get_weight loops: `for (uint256 i; i < 500; ++i)`; _get_total nests loop over gauge types
proof_of_signal.pattern: bounded but potentially large multi-loop catch-up with nested gauge-type iteration

preconditions: Sufficient history gap exists to exercise near-maximum iterations
Caller triggers a write function that must catch up (no early break)
Gas limit constraints make function revert

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: gas-heavy catch-up loops callable by anyone
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: DoS of LendingLedger.update_market due to dependency on gauge_relative_weight_write
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no incremental checkpointing incentives or per-call partial progress guarantees beyond 500-week cap

analysis_questions: What is the maximum n_gauge_types expected and how does it affect worst-case gas?
Can attackers amplify cost by creating many types? (requires governance, so config-dependent)
Does LendingLedger have fallback behavior if gauge_relative_weight_write fails? (none)
validation_requirements: Gas benchmark with time lag near 500 weeks and varying n_gauge_types
Simulate update_market calling gauge_relative_weight_write under worst-case lag

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After long inactivity, attacker calls gauge_relative_weight_write on a popular market to force heavy catch-up and potentially cause reverts for others trying to update/claim until someone successfully pays the gas cost (or until impossible).
prerequisites: Extended inactivity or misconfiguration increasing lag/types

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect: blocks reward accounting updates, may stall claims/market sync paths depending on integrations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of markets whose updates depend on gauge controller writes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational (call checkpoint more frequently) if still within gas; otherwise may require contract changes

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No performance/regression tests for catch-up worst-case

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: worst-case gas for loops and nested type iteration
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor revert rates on checkpoint and gauge_relative_weight_write

validation_result.mechanism: unbounded catch-up loops with nested storage writes
validation_result.impact_summary: GaugeController checkpointing functions can exceed block gas limits after extended inactivity combined with multiple gauge types, permanently bricking gauge weight writes and downstream reward accounting in LendingLedger.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that _get_total(), _get_sum(), _get_type_weight(), and _get_weight() each loop up to 500 iterations performing storage writes. The critical issue is that _get_total() nests these: it first calls _get_sum() and _get_type_weight() for EACH gauge type (each doing up to 500 iterations of storage writes), then does its own 500-iteration loop with an inner loop over all gauge types. The gas cost scales as O(n_gauge_types * weeks_behind) storage writes. A Point struct occupies 2 storage slots (bias and slope as uint256), so each write to a new Point costs ~40,000 gas. Even with just 2 gauge types and ~150 weeks (~2.9 years) of inactivity, the total gas for _get_sum alone would be 2 * 150 * ~42,000 = ~12.6M gas, plus _get_type_weight and the main loop, easily approaching or exceeding a 30M block gas limit. With more gauge types (5-10), the threshold for bricking drops dramatically. There is no partial-progress mechanism: if the catch-up exceeds the gas limit, it always reverts, and there is no way to checkpoint incrementally across multiple transactions.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence directly matches the hypothesis. The loops at _get_sum (up to 500 iterations, writing Point structs to storage), _get_type_weight (up to 500 iterations, writing uint256 to storage), and _get_total (calling both for each type, then its own 500-iteration loop) are all confirmed in GaugeController.sol. The unprivileged entry points checkpoint(), checkpoint_gauge(), and gauge_relative_weight_write() all trigger these paths. LendingLedger.update_market() calls gauge_relative_weight_write(), creating a dependency chain that would be broken if GaugeController becomes uncallable.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Calls _get_sum and _get_type_weight for each gauge type (each can loop 500 times with storage writes), then has its own 500-iteration loop with inner loop over all types. Gas complexity is O(n_gauge_types * weeks_behind) storage writes, with no partial progress mechanism.
Each iteration writes a Point struct (2 storage slots) to a new mapping key, costing ~40,000+ gas per iteration. With 500 iterations, a single call can cost 20M+ gas in storage writes alone.

Publicly callable function that triggers both _get_weight (up to 500 iterations) and _get_total (which cascades into _get_sum and _get_type_weight for each gauge type). This is the entry point called by LendingLedger.update_market().
Calls gauge_relative_weight_write inside a while loop. If gauge_relative_weight_write reverts due to gas exhaustion, update_market and all dependent functions (sync_ledger, claim) are blocked.

calling_sequence: 1. Anyone calls gauge_relative_weight_write(_gauge, _time) or LendingLedger.update_market calls it
2. _get_weight(_gauge) loops up to 500 times, writing Point structs to storage
3. _get_total() is called, which first loops over n_gauge_types calling _get_sum() and _get_type_weight() for each
4. _get_sum(gauge_type) loops up to 500 times per type, writing Point structs (2 slots each) to storage
5. _get_type_weight(gauge_type) loops up to 500 times per type, writing uint256 to storage
6. _get_total() then does its own loop of up to 500 iterations, each with inner loop over n_gauge_types doing reads plus 1 storage write
7. Total gas: O(n_gauge_types * weeks_behind) storage writes, each costing 20,000-40,000 gas
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: _get_total calls _get_sum and _get_type_weight for EACH gauge type before its own loop. If there are T types and W weeks behind, the storage writes are approximately: T*(W writes for _get_sum at ~40k gas each) + T*(W writes for _get_type_weight at ~20k gas each) + W*(1 write at ~20k gas) + _get_weight W writes at ~40k gas. Total ≈ W * (60k*T + 60k) gas. With T=3 types and W=100 weeks: 100 * (180k + 60k) = 24M gas, approaching typical 30M limits. With T=5 and W=100: 100 * (300k + 60k) = 36M gas, exceeding limits.

deployment_assumptions_checked: time_total/time_sum/time_weight/time_type_weight lag significantly behind block.timestamp (e.g., weeks/months of inactivity)
n_gauge_types is large enough that _get_total() inner loop becomes costly
Block gas limit is insufficient for catch-up worst case
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.sol creates GaugeController with no automated checkpointing setup, no limits on gauge type count, and no operational procedures for regular checkpointing. The constructor sets time_total to the current week-floored timestamp but makes no provision for keeping it up to date. There is no mechanism in any deployed contract to enforce regular checkpointing or bound the number of gauge types.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is valid as a code defect but has limited active attack surface. An attacker cannot create gauge types (governance only) and cannot prevent others from calling checkpoint(). However, the defect manifests naturally: if the system goes through a period of low activity (no checkpoints for weeks/months) with multiple gauge types, the catch-up cost can exceed gas limits. Once this threshold is crossed, the system is permanently bricked with no recovery mechanism short of contract redeployment or gas limit increase. The downstream impact is real: LendingLedger.update_market depends on gauge_relative_weight_write, so sync_ledger (called from LiquidityGauge._afterTokenTransfer) and claim() would all revert, freezing user funds in gauges and blocking reward claims.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If the catch-up gas exceeds block gas limits, the GaugeController becomes permanently non-functional for write operations. This cascades to LendingLedger: update_market reverts, sync_ledger reverts (blocking deposits and withdrawals via LiquidityGauge), and claim reverts (blocking reward claims). User funds in LiquidityGauge contracts become locked since withdrawUnderlying calls _burn which triggers _afterTokenTransfer → sync_ledger → update_market → gauge_relative_weight_write. Recovery would require contract redeployment or chain-level gas limit increases.
recommended_mitigations: Implement incremental/partial checkpointing that saves progress across transactions, allowing catch-up to span multiple blocks
Reduce the loop bound from 500 to a smaller value (e.g., 52 for ~1 year) to limit worst-case gas
Add a governance function to force-update timestamps without filling history when gaps are too large
Consider bounding n_gauge_types or adding gas estimation checks before expensive loops
Add an emergency bypass in LendingLedger that allows operations to proceed without gauge weight updates when the controller is stuck
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts. 2. Add 5 gauge types via governance. 3. Add a gauge for each type. 4. Use vm.warp to advance time by 200 weeks without calling any checkpoint functions. 5. Attempt to call checkpoint() or gauge_relative_weight_write() and measure gas consumption. 6. Verify that with sufficient time advancement and types, the call exceeds the block gas limit and reverts. 7. Verify that LendingLedger.update_market also fails as a result.
uncertainties: Exact block gas limit on Canto (likely 30M but could differ)
Exact storage operation gas costs depend on EVM version and access patterns (cold vs warm)
Whether governance would realistically add many gauge types
Whether external monitoring/bots would keep checkpoints current in practice
The scenario requires extended inactivity which may be unlikely on an active chain
additional_testing_needed: Gas benchmarking with varying weeks_behind (50, 100, 200, 500) and n_gauge_types (1, 2, 5, 10)
End-to-end test showing LendingLedger functions reverting when GaugeController catch-up exceeds gas
Test with Canto-specific block gas limits
Verify that partial checkpoint calls cannot be used as a workaround (they can't - each write function restarts the full catch-up)
